items.title: US-Iran deal: Key questions remain over status of Strait of Hormuz
items.description: The reopening of the Strait of Hormuz is at the heart of the Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) signed by the United States and Iran. Under the terms of the deal, the US will immediately lift its blockade of Iranian ports. The Islamic Republic, for its part, will reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and charge no toll for commercial vessels for a period of 60 days. However, the presence of sea mines in the vital waterway could be a cause for concern. Our correspondent Reza Sayah reports from the Strait of Hormuz.

items.title: Paris reimagined: Exploring the city's past, present and future through three iconic landmarks
items.description: In this edition, we discover how some of Paris's most famous landmarks are being transformed by technology, culture and contemporary art. We travel beneath the Eiffel Tower with a new virtual reality experience that brings the monument's history to life, celebrate the 20th anniversary of the Quai Branly Museum and its extraordinary collection of art and artefacts from around the world, and cross the Pont Neuf as French artist JR turns the city's oldest bridge into a spectacular cave-inspired installation. Together, these three stories offer a fresh perspective on Paris and the many ways it continues to reinvent itself.

items.description: Nadia Massih is pleased to welcome Manuel Muñiz, Lawyer and Provost of IE University in Madrid and a Professor of Practice of International Relations. The real story of the summit, Muñiz argues, was transatlantic strain. With disagreements stretching from trade and climate to NATO, Ukraine and Russia, Muñiz suggests that simply keeping the alliance together was itself an achievement. As he puts it, “you would be hard pressed to find a moment in the last decades where there were so many disagreements on the table across the Atlantic.”

items.title: Report: Russia's nuclear-powered 'Skyfall' missile is dirty and dangerous
items.description: MIT researchers think they've worked out exactly how Russia's Burevestnik nuclear-powered missile flies. "It's almost certainly a terrible idea," one analyst said. "But it's not an impossible idea."

items.title: EV public charger shortage? The data shows drivers rarely queue to charge
items.description: It's often claimed Australia has an EV public charger shortage, but analysis of exclusive data shows most fast charger sites are not congested.

items.title: How a feel-good German song is taking over social media

items.description: South Africa leave it late to snatch a 1-1 draw with Czechia, as Group A is blown wide open in Atlanta. Meanwhile, US talisman Christian Pulisic doesn't train ahead of his side's clash with the Socceroos. Follow live.

items.title: New exhibitions for Anish Kapoor: Art on the edge

items.title: Keyamo speaks on China-Enugu cargo flights
items.description: The aviation minister says major airport developments in Abia and Enugu are expected to boost trade, ease cargo movement and open new international routes for the South-east. The post Keyamo speaks on China-Enugu cargo flights appeared first on Premium Times Nigeria .

items.title: How Germany and Poland rebooted relations 35 years ago
items.description: When Germany and Poland signed the Treaty on Good Neighborliness, Friendship and Cooperation in June 1991, they chose partnership over enmity and became close NATO and EU allies. But it hasn't all been plain sailing.

items.title: World Cup: Cape Verde hero and Iran suffer US travel woes
items.description: After starring in Cape Verde's draw with Spain, goalkeeper Vozinha shared his sadness that an expensive travel bond stopped his mother from attending. On the same day, Iran's coach said his team were being "oppressed."

items.title: CRISPR gene-editing for crops: Precision tool or new risk?

items.description: Scores of drones targeted Moscow overnight in what was Ukraine's largest attack on the Russian capital.

items.title: As a result of Iran war, Gulf states 'diversifying defensively'
items.description: As the Strait of Hormuz reopens and talks between the US and Iran start up again, how are Gulf states emerging from the Middle East war? Previously thought of as the safe haven of the Middle East, the surprise US-Israeli attack on Iran has left them reeling, and triggered a search to diversify defensively. FRANCE 24's Angela Diffley explains.

items.title: Ukraine hits Moscow with ‘large-scale’ attack

In a post on X, Ukrainian President Volodymyr #Zelensky said #Kyiv also struck targets in #Russia's Rostov region and in the occupied territories of Ukraine.

items.description: Over 1,73,200 people have been wounded since the start of the war, which was ignited by the Hamas-led October 7, 2023 attack into Israel. That attack killed some 1,200 people and took 251 others hostage

items.title: Ghana pushes for concrete slavery reparations
items.description: In today's edition, leaders from Africa and the Caribbean meet in Ghana for a landmark conference on reparatory justice. Also, Taiwan has accused Kenya of bowing to Chinese pressure to sideline Taiwanese officials meant to take part in an international oceans forum that wrapped up in Mombasa. And at the Busuma camp in Burundi, refugees from DR Congo are fully behind their national team competing in the World Cup.

items.title: Is Russia heading for a fuel crisis?
items.description: Russia's fuel output is falling after Ukrainian drone strikes on refineries. Seasonal spikes in gasoline and diesel demand could push the system into a deeper crisis, experts say.
